meta.role: Software Engineer, Front-End — Superhuman / Grammarly Berlin
meta.instructions: Each challenge has a difficulty, time budget, required criteria, and bonus criteria. When assessing a submission, score each required criterion as PASS/FAIL and each bonus as ACHIEVED/MISSED. Then give an overall grade: STRONG PASS / PASS / BORDERLINE / FAIL. Always comment on: code quality, TypeScript correctness, accessibility, edge case handling, and what a Grammarly/Superhuman interviewer would specifically notice.

challenges.title: Character counter with live feedback
challenges.prompt: Build a textarea component with a live character counter. The counter changes colour based on thresholds. Submit is disabled when the limit is exceeded.
required: Controlled textarea with a configurable maxLength prop
Counter turns amber at 80% of limit, red at 100%
Submit button is disabled when count exceeds limit
Counter is announced to screen readers via aria-live='polite'
bonus: Smooth CSS colour transition on threshold change
Show remaining vs used count depending on which is smaller
Accept a formatter prop to support word count instead of character count
challenges.interviewer_focus: Do they reach for useState (correct) or useRef? Do they remember aria-live? Are props typed correctly in TypeScript?

challenges.title: Toggle switch component
challenges.prompt: Build an accessible toggle switch that supports both controlled and uncontrolled modes.
required: Controlled (value + onChange) and uncontrolled (defaultChecked) modes
role='switch', aria-checked, focusable via Tab
Space and Enter toggle the switch
Disabled state with correct aria-disabled and visual treatment
bonus: CSS-only knob transition animation
Label position prop: left or right
forwardRef support
challenges.interviewer_focus: ARIA knowledge, not CSS skill. Does the candidate know role='switch'? Do they use a button element (correct) or a div with onclick (wrong)?

challenges.title: Debounced search input
challenges.prompt: Search input that debounces keystrokes and fires a mock async fetch. Show loading, results, and empty states.
required: Custom useDebounce hook with configurable delay
AbortController cancels in-flight requests on new keystroke
Three distinct UI states: loading, results, empty
No stale results shown after cancellation
bonus: Highlight matched substring in results
Keyboard navigation through results list
Results cached per query in a useRef map
challenges.interviewer_focus: The stale request problem is the key test. AbortController + useEffect cleanup is the correct answer. This is directly relevant to Grammarly's real-time suggestion pipeline.

challenges.title: Accordion / FAQ component
challenges.prompt: Accessible accordion supporting single-expand and multi-expand modes. Full keyboard navigation per the ARIA APG pattern.
required: Single-expand and multi-expand modes via prop
ARIA: role='region', aria-expanded, aria-controls, aria-labelledby
Arrow keys navigate between headers; Enter/Space toggles
Smooth height animation on open/close
bonus: Height animation using the CSS grid-template-rows trick (0fr to 1fr)
defaultOpen prop for initial state
Focus first item on mount
challenges.interviewer_focus: Knowledge of the ARIA APG accordion pattern without googling. Use of grid-template-rows for animation shows CSS depth.

challenges.title: Pill / tag input
challenges.prompt: Input that converts typed text into removable pill tags on Enter or comma. Used in email CC/BCC fields.
required: Enter or comma confirms a tag; Backspace removes last tag when input is empty
Duplicate tags rejected with inline error
Each pill has a keyboard-accessible remove button
Controlled: value (string[]) + onChange prop
bonus: Email validation with per-pill error state
Pasting a comma-separated list creates multiple pills
Max tags limit with user feedback
challenges.interviewer_focus: This is a Superhuman Mail pattern. Focus management is the tricky part: after removing a pill, where does focus go? Expect a follow-up question on this.

challenges.title: Sortable, filterable data table
challenges.prompt: Table of 100+ rows with client-side sort by any column and text filter. Performance matters.
required: useMemo for filtered and sorted dataset
Debounced filter input
Sort indicator (asc/desc/none) per column header
Correct table semantics: thead, th with scope, caption
bonus: Multi-column sort with priority order
Column resizing
Virtual scrolling for 10k+ rows
challenges.interviewer_focus: Correct useMemo deps. Interviewer will ask 'what happens at 10k rows?' — candidate should proactively raise virtualization before being asked.

challenges.title: Autocomplete / combobox
challenges.prompt: Accessible combobox with typeahead. Fetches suggestions asynchronously. Full keyboard navigation per ARIA APG combobox pattern.
required: role='combobox', aria-expanded, aria-activedescendant correctly wired
Arrow keys cycle options; Enter selects; Escape closes
Async fetch with debounce and AbortController
Matched substring highlighted in option text
bonus: Grouped options with role='group'
Free-text entry allowed (not forced to select from list)
Recent selections persisted to localStorage
challenges.interviewer_focus: aria-activedescendant is the key attribute — focus stays on the input while screen readers track the active option. Getting this right without looking it up is impressive.

challenges.title: Toast / notification system
challenges.prompt: Global toast system with a context + hook API. Toasts auto-dismiss, stack, and pause on hover.
required: useToast() hook returning { toast, dismiss }
Stack up to N toasts; oldest auto-dismissed when limit reached
Auto-dismiss timer pauses while mouse is over the toast
Four types: success, error, info, warning with distinct visuals
bonus: Animate in from bottom, animate out on dismiss
Position prop: top/bottom, left/center/right
Progress bar showing time remaining before dismiss
challenges.interviewer_focus: Architecture question: where does state live? Context + useReducer is the correct answer. Candidate should explain this choice, not just implement it.

challenges.title: Infinite scroll feed
challenges.prompt: Feed that loads more items on scroll using IntersectionObserver. Show skeleton loaders while fetching.
required: IntersectionObserver on a sentinel element at the list bottom
Skeleton placeholder rows during loading
Error state with a retry button
Loading guard (ref or state) prevents duplicate fetches
bonus: Pull-to-refresh on mobile
Cursor-based pagination rather than page number
Optimistic item removal
challenges.interviewer_focus: The trap is scroll event listeners on window. IntersectionObserver is the correct answer. Cleanup in useEffect return is critical.

challenges.title: Keyboard command palette
challenges.prompt: Cmd+K command palette with fuzzy search, keyboard navigation, and focus trapping. Core to Superhuman's product identity.
required: Cmd/Ctrl+K opens; Escape closes
Fuzzy filter matches non-consecutive characters
Arrow up/down navigates; Enter executes command
Focus trapped inside modal while open
bonus: Grouped command sections
Recent commands surface first
Per-command keyboard shortcut badge displayed
challenges.interviewer_focus: This is Superhuman's exact UX. Discuss focus trapping, role='dialog' + aria-modal. Fuzzy matching: score by match position. Keyboard events must not fire when focus is in a text input.

challenges.title: Inline text annotation / highlight
challenges.prompt: Given a string and annotation ranges, render text with highlights. Clicking a highlight shows a popover with the annotation message.
required: Algorithm splits text into plain and annotated segments using offset ranges
Each annotation has: range [start, end], type (error/warning/suggestion), message
Click annotated span opens popover with message and dismiss action
Overlapping ranges handled without crashing
bonus: Tab between annotations by keyboard; Enter opens popover
Multiple types with distinct underline colours/styles
Accept and reject actions in popover update the text
challenges.interviewer_focus: The core Grammarly challenge. Walk through the segment-splitting algorithm out loud before coding. Overlapping ranges are the hard case — have a strategy ready.

challenges.title: Drag-and-drop list reordering
challenges.prompt: Reorderable list using native HTML5 drag-and-drop API (no library). Full keyboard alternative for accessibility.
required: Native drag events: dragstart, dragover, drop, dragend
Visual drop target indicator during drag
State updated correctly on drop
Keyboard alternative: select an item, then arrow keys move it
bonus: FLIP animation on reorder
Multi-item drag selection
Touch event support
challenges.interviewer_focus: dragover.preventDefault() is required to enable drop. The keyboard alternative is non-trivial — it requires a separate 'selected-for-move' state. Grading on whether a11y is an afterthought or built-in.

challenges.title: Real-time collaborative cursor display
challenges.prompt: Textarea where simulated other users' cursor positions are shown as coloured indicators with name labels.
required: Mock WebSocket or setInterval simulates other users moving cursors
Cursors overlaid at correct character offset positions
Each user has a unique colour and name label
Cursor positions update smoothly without flicker
bonus: Smooth cursor movement animation
Name badge fades after inactivity timeout
Selection ranges shown, not just caret positions
challenges.interviewer_focus: The hard part: mapping character offsets to pixel coordinates in a textarea requires a mirror div technique. Candidate should identify this problem and explain the approach before coding.

challenges.title: Virtual list renderer (windowing from scratch)
challenges.prompt: Render 100k items in a scrollable list with only visible items in the DOM. Build without react-virtual or similar libraries.
required: Visible window calculated from scrollTop + containerHeight + itemHeight
Only visible items + overscan buffer rendered in DOM
Spacer div above and below maintains total scroll height
Scroll position preserved across re-renders
bonus: Variable row heights with position caching
scrollToItem(index) API
Sticky section headers
challenges.interviewer_focus: Start with fixed-height items — the math is straightforward. Key insight: explain the spacer div technique unprompted. Candidate should state time complexity O(visible) vs O(n).

challenges.title: Rich text mention system (@mention)
challenges.prompt: Textarea where typing @ opens a user picker. Selecting a user inserts a styled, atomic mention chip.
required: @ character triggers picker at cursor position
Picker filters users as you type after @
Selected user inserted as a styled inline element
Mention chip is atomic: Backspace deletes the whole chip, not character by character
bonus: Picker positioned relative to cursor coordinates, not textarea corner
Keyboard navigation in picker without losing typing context
Mention IDs extractable from the final value
challenges.interviewer_focus: contenteditable is complex. A simpler simulation with a textarea + overlay is acceptable — explain the tradeoff. Making the chip atomic is the hard constraint.

challenges.title: Writing suggestion overlay (Grammarly-style)
challenges.prompt: Full Grammarly-style interface: textarea with underlined issue highlights, click to see suggestion card, accept replaces the text.
required: Mirror div technique: overlay positioned exactly over textarea
Underlines rendered in overlay; textarea has transparent background
Suggestion card shows: original text, replacement, explanation
Accept splices replacement at correct offset and removes highlight
bonus: Multiple suggestion types with different underline styles
Undo support after accepting a suggestion
Keyboard: Tab through suggestions; Enter to accept
challenges.interviewer_focus: The mirror div architecture must be explained before coding. After accepting a suggestion, all subsequent offset ranges must be recalculated — this is the hard part.

challenges.title: Undo/redo history manager
challenges.prompt: Custom useHistory hook with undo/redo. Applied to a text editor. Coalesces rapid keystrokes into single history entries.
required: useHistory<T>(initial) returns { state, set, undo, redo, canUndo, canRedo }
set() clears the future stack
Entries within 500ms are coalesced into one history step
Cmd+Z and Cmd+Shift+Z keyboard shortcuts
bonus: Maximum history size with oldest-entry eviction
Named history entries for a visual timeline
Branching history (tree structure, not linear stack)
challenges.interviewer_focus: The coalescing logic is the interesting part — timestamp comparison. Branching history is genuinely hard and worth raising as 'where I'd take this next.'

challenges.title: Collaborative presence indicator
challenges.prompt: Show which users are currently active. Stack up to 3 avatars; overflow as +N. Tooltip shows all names.
required: Simulated presence events via setInterval with join/leave
Stacked avatar overlap with z-index layering
First 3 avatars shown; remaining as +N badge
+N badge has accessible tooltip showing all names on hover/focus
bonus: Animated entry and exit for users joining/leaving
Per-user current-location indicator
Idle state after configurable inactivity timeout
challenges.interviewer_focus: Stacking CSS: negative margin-left + z-index. Tooltip a11y: role='tooltip' + aria-describedby. FLIP technique for join/leave animation.

challenges.title: Resizable split panel layout
challenges.prompt: Two panels with a draggable divider. Min/max constraints. Keyboard resize. Persist to localStorage.
required: Pointer events (not mouse events) for cross-device drag support
Min panel width constraints enforced during drag
Keyboard: focus divider, arrow keys resize
Layout persisted to localStorage and restored on mount
bonus: Snap to preset ratios at drag threshold
Collapse panel on double-click
Vertical split orientation via prop
challenges.interviewer_focus: setPointerCapture ensures drag works even when cursor leaves the divider. Keyboard interaction requires role='separator' + aria-valuenow + aria-valuemin/max.

challenges.title: Email thread reader (Superhuman-style)
challenges.prompt: Stripped-down email client: message list + detail pane. Navigable entirely by keyboard with vim-style shortcuts.
required: j/k to navigate message list
Enter/o to open message in detail pane
r to open reply textarea with focus
e to archive current message and auto-advance to next
bonus: ? opens keyboard shortcut reference overlay
/ focuses search input; Escape returns to list
Unread count badge; message marked read on open
challenges.interviewer_focus: This is Superhuman's actual product. Keyboard events must not fire when focus is in a text input. Show philosophical understanding of keyboard-first design, not just implementation.

challenges.title: Custom modal / dialog with focus trap
challenges.prompt: A reusable Modal component with a proper focus trap, scroll lock, and accessible overlay dismiss.
required: Focus trapped inside modal: Tab and Shift+Tab cycle only focusable elements inside
Escape key closes modal
Clicking overlay backdrop closes modal
role='dialog', aria-modal='true', aria-labelledby wired to the heading
bonus: Return focus to the trigger element on close
Body scroll locked while modal is open
Animation: fade-in on open, fade-out on close
challenges.interviewer_focus: Focus trap implementation is the core test. Candidate should query all focusable elements, intercept Tab keydown, and wrap focus from last to first. Returning focus on close is the a11y detail that separates good from great.

challenges.title: Multi-step form wizard
challenges.prompt: A wizard component that manages multi-step form state, validation per step, and progress indication.
required: Step state managed centrally; each step validates before advancing
Back navigation preserves filled values
Progress indicator shows current step and completion
Final step shows a review summary before submit
bonus: URL hash or query param encodes current step for deep linking
Animated step transition (slide or fade)
Step components are injected as children (compound component pattern)
challenges.interviewer_focus: Architecture question: does candidate reach for a single flat state object or per-step state? Ask about what happens if a user edits step 1 after completing step 3.

challenges.title: Live markdown preview
challenges.prompt: Split-pane editor where the left side accepts markdown input and the right side renders a live HTML preview.
required: Live preview updates as user types (debounced)
Basic markdown parsed: headings, bold, italic, code, links, lists
Rendered HTML sanitised before insertion (no XSS)
Scroll positions synchronised between panes
bonus: Syntax highlighting in the editor pane
Toolbar with formatting buttons that insert markdown syntax
Export to HTML or download as .md file
challenges.interviewer_focus: XSS sanitisation is the non-obvious requirement — DOMPurify or manual escaping. Scroll sync is the UX challenge. Ask candidate to explain their parsing approach vs using a library.

challenges.title: JSON tree viewer
challenges.prompt: Collapsible tree view for arbitrary JSON data. Colour-coded by type. Expandable/collapsible at any depth.
required: Recursive rendering of objects, arrays, primitives
Type-based colour coding: string, number, boolean, null
Expand/collapse at any node; expand-all and collapse-all controls
Key count shown on collapsed nodes (e.g. {3 keys})
bonus: In-place editing of primitive values
Search/filter that highlights matching keys or values
Copy subtree to clipboard as JSON
challenges.interviewer_focus: Recursive component design. Does candidate handle circular reference detection? What is the TypeScript type for an arbitrary JSON value? (type JSONValue = string | number | boolean | null | JSONValue[] | {[key: string]: JSONValue})

challenges.title: Tooltip system
challenges.prompt: A Tooltip component that positions itself intelligently relative to its trigger. Accessible and keyboard-friendly.
required: Appears on hover and focus; hides on blur and mouseout
Positions itself to stay within the viewport (flip when near edge)
role='tooltip' on the popover; aria-describedby on the trigger
Delay prop before show; immediate hide
bonus: Arrow/caret pointing toward trigger
Rich content (not just text string) via children prop
Animation: fade + subtle scale on appear
challenges.interviewer_focus: The viewport-collision detection is the hard part — getBoundingClientRect + window dimensions. Candidate should handle both sides: CSS makes it look right; ARIA makes it accessible.

challenges.title: Custom dropdown / select
challenges.prompt: A styled replacement for the native <select> element. Full keyboard navigation per ARIA listbox pattern.
required: role='listbox' on the dropdown, role='option' on each item
Arrow keys navigate options; Enter/Space selects; Escape closes
aria-selected on active option; aria-activedescendant on trigger
Closes on outside click; repositions to stay in viewport
bonus: Type-ahead: typing a letter jumps to matching option
Multi-select mode with checkboxes
Grouped options with group labels
challenges.interviewer_focus: The ARIA listbox pattern is more complex than most candidates realise. The distinction between aria-selected (persisted selection) and aria-activedescendant (current keyboard focus) is the key.

challenges.title: Diff viewer
challenges.prompt: Side-by-side or unified view showing the difference between two strings line-by-line. Additions in green, deletions in red.
required: Implement a basic LCS (longest common subsequence) diff algorithm
Line-by-line diff with added/removed/unchanged classification
Side-by-side and unified view modes
Line numbers shown in both views
bonus: Word-level diff within changed lines
Collapse unchanged regions (show only ±N lines of context)
Character-level diff highlighting within changed words
challenges.interviewer_focus: This is the algorithmic challenge in a UI wrapper. Candidate should recognise and implement LCS before reaching for a library. Time complexity should be O(n*m) — ask them to confirm.

challenges.title: Rate limiter / throttle hook
challenges.prompt: useThrottle and useDebounce hooks. Demonstrate both with a live example showing the difference in behaviour.
required: useDebounce: delays execution until input stops for N ms
useThrottle: executes at most once per N ms
Both hooks clean up timers in useEffect return
Demo component visualises both behaviours side by side
bonus: useThrottle accepts leading and trailing options
useDebounce returns a cancel function
Visual timeline showing when each hook fires relative to input events
challenges.interviewer_focus: Candidates often confuse debounce and throttle. The visual demo is the communication test — can they explain the difference clearly? Cleanup on unmount is the correctness test.

challenges.title: File upload with drag-and-drop
challenges.prompt: File upload zone that accepts drag-and-drop or click-to-browse. Shows per-file upload progress and errors.
required: Drag-over visual feedback on the drop zone
Simulated async upload per file with progress percentage
Per-file state: pending, uploading, done, error
File type and size validation with inline error messages
bonus: Multiple file upload with concurrent uploads (max N simultaneous)
Retry failed uploads
Image thumbnail preview for image files
challenges.interviewer_focus: dragenter/dragleave fire on child elements too — the bubble-up problem. Candidate should handle this with a counter or by checking relatedTarget. The concurrent upload logic is the architectural challenge.

challenges.title: Countdown timer
challenges.prompt: A countdown timer component with start, pause, reset. Accurate even when tab is hidden.
required: Start, pause, resume, and reset controls
Displays MM:SS format
Does not drift: uses Date.now() delta, not tick counting
Visual urgency: changes colour in last 10 seconds
bonus: Audible alert (Web Audio API) at zero
Tab title shows remaining time when running
Configurable initial duration via prop
challenges.interviewer_focus: The drift problem is the test: naive setInterval counting drifts. Using Date.now() to calculate elapsed time on each tick is the correct approach. Most candidates get this wrong without prompting.

challenges.title: Virtualized autocomplete with 10k options
challenges.prompt: Extend challenge #7 (autocomplete) to handle 10,000 options without performance degradation. The dropdown list must be virtualised.
required: Dropdown option list is virtualised (only visible options in DOM)
Fuzzy filter runs without blocking the main thread (consider useDeferredValue or Web Worker)
Arrow key navigation works correctly with virtualised list
aria-activedescendant still correct for screen readers
bonus: Web Worker for filtering to guarantee 60fps
Scroll the virtualised list to keep active option in view
Loading state while filter result is deferred
challenges.interviewer_focus: This combines challenge 7 with challenge 14. The intersection of accessibility and virtualisation is genuinely hard — aria-activedescendant requires the active option element to exist in the DOM.

challenges.title: Spreadsheet-like editable grid
challenges.prompt: A grid of editable cells navigable entirely by keyboard, like a mini spreadsheet.
required: Arrow keys move between cells
Enter or F2 puts a cell into edit mode; Escape cancels; Enter confirms
Tab moves to the next cell; Shift+Tab to previous
Copy (Cmd+C) copies selected cell value to clipboard
bonus: Multi-cell selection with Shift+Arrow
Column and row headers that are resizable
Paste from clipboard into selected cell or range
challenges.interviewer_focus: Cell focus management is the core challenge. The distinction between 'cell is focused' (keyboard navigation) and 'cell is in edit mode' (text input active) requires careful state design.

challenges.title: Pagination component
challenges.prompt: A pagination component that displays page numbers with intelligent truncation for large page counts.
required: Always show first, last, current, and ±1 adjacent pages
Ellipsis for gaps: 1 … 4 5 6 … 20
Previous/Next buttons with disabled states at boundaries
Keyboard accessible: all controls reachable by Tab and activatable by Enter/Space
bonus: Page size selector (10/25/50/100 per page)
Jump-to-page input
URL search param integration (e.g. ?page=3)
challenges.interviewer_focus: The truncation algorithm is the interesting part — most candidates hardcode it poorly. A clean data structure approach (generate an array of page items including 'ellipsis' tokens) is the right abstraction.

challenges.title: Skeleton loader system
challenges.prompt: A composable skeleton loading system. Skeleton components match the shape of the real content they replace.
required: Skeleton primitive: block, circle, text line
Animated shimmer effect via CSS
Composable: skeleton cards that match actual card layout
Controlled by a loading prop — swaps between skeleton and real content
bonus: Reduced-motion: shimmer disabled when prefers-reduced-motion
Theme-aware: shimmer adapts to dark mode
Skeleton inferred from real component shape automatically
challenges.interviewer_focus: prefers-reduced-motion support is the a11y signal. Composability is the design signal. A candidate who builds one generic <Skeleton> component and composes it is thinking the right way.

challenges.title: Keyboard shortcut manager
challenges.prompt: A useKeyboardShortcut hook and a ShortcutProvider context that manages shortcut registration, conflict detection, and scoping.
required: useKeyboardShortcut(keys, handler, options) hook
Shortcuts scoped to component mount (deregister on unmount)
Conflict detection: warn in dev if two handlers claim the same shortcut
Shortcuts disabled when focus is inside a text input unless explicitly opted in
bonus: Shortcut sequences (e.g. g then i for Go to Inbox)
ShortcutsReference component that renders all active shortcuts
Platform-aware: show Cmd on Mac, Ctrl on Windows
challenges.interviewer_focus: The text-input guard is the most commonly missed requirement. The scoping model (component-level registration vs global) is the architecture question. Candidate should discuss both approaches.

challenges.title: Rich text editor toolbar
challenges.prompt: A formatting toolbar for a contenteditable region. Bold, italic, underline, link insertion — using the Selection and Range APIs directly.
required: Bold, italic, underline via document.execCommand or Selection/Range API
Link insertion: prompt for URL, wrap selection in <a>
Toolbar buttons reflect current format state (bold button active when cursor is in bold text)
Keyboard shortcuts: Cmd+B, Cmd+I, Cmd+U, Cmd+K
bonus: Heading levels (H1/H2) applied to current block
Undo/redo integrated with the browser's native history
Custom serialisation: export to markdown or HTML string
challenges.interviewer_focus: document.execCommand is deprecated but still works — candidate should know this and mention alternatives (Selection/Range API or a library). queryCommandState() for toolbar active-state is the non-obvious requirement.

challenges.title: Image lazy loader with blur-up
challenges.prompt: An image component that loads a tiny placeholder first, then transitions to the full image with a blur-up effect on load.
required: Tiny base64 placeholder shown immediately
Full image loaded lazily via IntersectionObserver
CSS transition from blurred placeholder to sharp full image
Error state with fallback placeholder
bonus: loading='lazy' as a progressive enhancement alongside IntersectionObserver
Aspect ratio box prevents layout shift during load
srcSet support for responsive images
challenges.interviewer_focus: Layout shift prevention (aspect ratio box) is the performance signal. Using both IntersectionObserver and loading='lazy' as progressive enhancement shows web platform depth.

challenges.title: Chat interface with streaming responses
challenges.prompt: A chat UI where responses stream in token-by-token (simulated). New messages auto-scroll to bottom unless the user has scrolled up.
required: Streaming simulation: tokens appended every 50ms
Auto-scroll to bottom while streaming; pause auto-scroll if user scrolls up
Resume auto-scroll when user scrolls back to bottom
Message bubbles: user right-aligned, assistant left-aligned
bonus: Typing indicator (three animated dots) before first token arrives
Stop generation button cancels the stream
Code blocks in responses rendered with syntax highlighting
challenges.interviewer_focus: The auto-scroll logic is the tricky part. isAtBottom check uses scrollTop + clientHeight vs scrollHeight with a small threshold. Candidate must not auto-scroll when user has intentionally scrolled up to read earlier messages.

challenges.title: Colour theme picker
challenges.prompt: A colour theme switcher that applies a selected palette as CSS custom properties across the entire app.
required: At least 4 preset themes stored as CSS variable maps
Selected theme applied to :root via JavaScript
Preference persisted to localStorage
Accessible colour swatches: keyboard navigable, aria-pressed state, sufficient contrast
bonus: Custom colour picker for accent colour
Transition animation when switching themes
System-preference detection (prefers-color-scheme) as default
challenges.interviewer_focus: The architecture question: CSS custom properties on :root vs. a data-theme attribute. Candidate should know both approaches and explain why data-theme='dark' on the root element is generally preferable to JS toggling every variable.

challenges.title: Text statistics dashboard
challenges.prompt: A textarea with a live analytics panel showing: word count, sentence count, reading time, most frequent words, and readability score.
required: Word count, sentence count, character count (with and without spaces)
Estimated reading time (based on 200 WPM)
Top 5 most frequent non-stopword words
All stats debounced — not computed on every keystroke
bonus: Flesch-Kincaid readability score with plain-English label (Easy/Medium/Hard)
Visualise word frequency as a mini bar chart
Sentence-length visualisation: short/medium/long sentences highlighted in different colours
challenges.interviewer_focus: The stopword filtering is a good discussion point — hardcode a small list vs. import a library. The Flesch-Kincaid formula requires syllable counting, which is non-trivial in JavaScript — ask the candidate how they'd approach it.
